title: W3 A Functions
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 3A    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/8
            Two Standard Problems in Economics


Definition 1

 Given a utility function u : X → R, the consumer’s utility maxi-
 mization problem (UMP) is
               max u(x)    subject to   p · x ≤ m.
               x≥0




Definition 2

 Given a production function f : Y → R, the firm’s cost minimiza-
 tion problem (CMP) is
               min w · y   subject to    f (y) ≥ q.
               y≥0




                                                                    2/8
              Two Standard Problems in Economics


 Definition 1

   Given a utility function u : X → R, the consumer’s utility maxi-
   mization problem (UMP) is
                max u(x)       subject to   p · x ≤ m.
                 x≥0




 Definition 2

   Given a production function f : Y → R, the firm’s cost minimiza-
   tion problem (CMP) is
                 min w · y     subject to   f (y) ≥ q.
                 y≥0




Optimal behavior relies on maximization or minimization problems
expressed in terms of functions.

                                                                      2/8
 Real-Valued Functions




                        3/8
                      Real-Valued Functions

Definition 3

 Let S be an arbitrary set. Say that f is a real-valued function if it
 maps S into a subset of R; i.e., f : S → R.




                                                                         4/8
                      Real-Valued Functions

Definition 3

 Let S be an arbitrary set. Say that f is a real-valued function if it
 maps S into a subset of R; i.e., f : S → R.


◦ S1 = {students enrolled in ECON 3202} and f1 (i) = height of i.

◦ S2 = R and f2 (x) = exp (x + 1).




                                                                         4/8
                        Real-Valued Functions

Definition 3

 Let S be an arbitrary set. Say that f is a real-valued function if it
 maps S into a subset of R; i.e., f : S → R.


◦ S1 = {students enrolled in ECON 3202} and f1 (i) = height of i.

◦ S2 = R and f2 (x) = exp (x + 1).
                                          √
◦ S3 = R2+ and f3 (z) = f3 (z1 , z2 ) =       z1 + z2 .

                        Real-Valued Functions

Definition 3

 maps S into a subset of R; i.e., f : S → R.


◦ S1 = {students enrolled in ECON 3202} and f1 (i) = height of i.

◦ S2 = R and f2 (x) = exp (x + 1).

                                                                         4/8
                        Increasing Functions on R


Definition 4

 Let I ⊆ R and f : I → R be a real-valued function.
  (a) f is weakly increasing if f (x) ≥ f (y) whenever x > y.

  (b) f is strictly increasing if f (x) > f (y) whenever x > y.


               f (x)                              f (x)



                                x                                 x




                                                                      5/8
                     Increasing Functions on R



Example 1

  (a) The function f (x) = ax + b is strictly increasing for a > 0 and
      strictly decreasing for a < 0.

 (b) The absolute value function is strictly increasing on R+ and
      strictly decreasing on R− .




                                                                         6/8
                     Increasing Functions on R



Example 1

  (a) The function f (x) = ax + b is strictly increasing for a > 0 and
      strictly decreasing for a < 0.

 (b) The absolute value function is strictly increasing on R+ and
      strictly decreasing on R− .

  (c) Let dz : R → R be the distance to z; i.e., dz (x) = |x − z|. It is
      strictly increasing for x > z and strictly decreasing for x < z.

 (d) ln(x) and exp(x) are strictly increasing functions.




                                                                           6/8
                        Increasing Functions on Rn


Notation For two vectors x, y in Rn ,
  ◦ x ≥ y if and only if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n;

  ◦ x > y if and only if xi > yi for all i = 1, . . . , n.




                                                             7/8
                        Increasing Functions on Rn


Notation For two vectors x, y in Rn ,
  ◦ x ≥ y if and only if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n;

  ◦ x > y if and only if xi > yi for all i = 1, . . . , n.

Consider a real-valued function f : Rn → R.

 Definition 5

    (a) f is weakly increasing if f (x) ≥ f (y) whenever x ≥ y.

    (b) f is strictly increasing if f (x) > f (y) whenever x ≥ y, x ̸= y.

    (c) f is strictly increasing for strict differences if f (x) > f (y)
        whenever x > y.


Notice (b) =⇒ (a).



                                                                            7/8
                            Functions on Rn

Example 2

  (a) Let g(x1 , x2 ) = ln x1 + ln x2 defined on R2++ .

  (b) Let d0 : R2 → R+ be the Euclidean distance to 0.

  (c) Let dz : Rn → R+ be the Euclidean distance to z.

  (d) Let u(x1 , x2 ) = xα 1 α2           2
                         1 x2 defined on R++ .

  (e) Let v(x1 , x2 ) = min{x1 , x2 } defined on R2+ .

  (f) Let w(x1 , x2 ) = c1 x1 + c2 x2 defined on R2+ for c1 , c2 > 0.



Exercise 1

 Identify all weakly increasing, strictly increasing, and strictly increas-
 ing for strict differences functions in Example 2.
